perturbation.original_text: Volunteer agrees to maintain the confidentiality of all Confidential Information and not misuse, misappropriate, or disclose in writing, orally or b y electronic means, any Confidential Information, directly or indirectly, to any other person or use them in any way, either during the term of this Agreement or at any other time thereafter, except as is required in the course of service to the CCPDT.
perturbation.changed_text: Volunteer agrees to endeavor to maintain the confidentiality of all Confidential Information and to avoid misusing, misappropriating, or disclosing Confidential Information. Disclosure may occur if deemed necessary by the Volunteer, provided it is not grossly detrimental to the CCPDT.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'agrees to maintain' with 'agrees to endeavor to maintain' weakens the obligation. Adding 'Disclosure may occur if deemed necessary by the Volunteer' introduces a subjective exception that undermines the confidentiality agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text allows for subjective determination of disclosure necessity, potentially conflicting with the Trade Secrets Act which protects against unauthorized disclosure regardless of the volunteer's perception of necessity, if it constitutes misappropriation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 18 U.S. Code § 1832 - Theft of trade secrets | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 18 U.S. Code § 1832 - Theft of trade secrets U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) Whoever, with intent to convert a trade secret , that is related to a product or service used in or intended for use in interstate or foreign commerce, to the economic benefit of anyone other than the owner thereof, and intending or knowing that the offense will, injure any owner of that trade secret , knowingly— (1) steals, or without authorization appropriates, takes, carries away, or conceals, or by fraud, artifice, or deception obtains such information; (2) without authorization copies, duplicates, sketches, draws, photographs, downloads, uploads, alters, destroys, photocopies, replicates, transmits, delivers, sends, mails, communicates, or conveys such information; (3) receives, buys, or possesses such information, knowing the same to have been stolen or appropriated, obtained, or converted without authorization; (4) attempts to commit any offense described in paragraphs (1) through (3); or (5) conspires with one or more other persons to commit any offense described in paragraphs (1) through (3), and one or more of such persons do any act to effect the object of the conspiracy, shall, except as provided in subsection (b), be fined under this title or imprisoned not more than 10 years, or both. (b) Any organization that commits any offense described in subsection (a) shall be fined not more than the greater of $5,000,000 or 3 times the value of the stolen trade secret to the organization, including expenses for research and design and other costs of reproducing the trade secret that the organization has thereby avoided. (Added Pub. L. 104–294, title I, § 101(a) , Oct. 11, 1996 , 110 Stat. 3489 ; amended Pub. L. 112–236, § 2 , Dec. 28, 2012 , 126 Stat. 1627 ; Pub. L. 114–153, § 3(a)(1) , May 11, 2016 , 130 Stat. 382

Perhaps you wish to explore one of these other options? About Us Get Involved Events Community Publications Projects & Tools Last updated Mar 27, 2024
perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces 'agrees to maintain' with 'agrees to endeavor to maintain', which weakens the obligation to protect confidential information. More significantly, it adds 'Disclosure may occur if deemed necessary by the Volunteer, provided it is not grossly detrimental to the CCPDT,' introducing a subjective exception that undermines the confidentiality agreement. This directly contradicts the Trade Secrets Act, as referenced by the LLM's Law Explanation, which protects against unauthorized disclosure. Scraped Law Snippet 1, 18 U.S. Code § 1832, defines the theft of trade secrets and prohibits unauthorized actions such as communicating or conveying such information. The modified text allows the volunteer to disclose information if they deem it necessary, which directly conflicts with the statute's prohibition of unauthorized disclosure, creating a legal contradiction, as it potentially allows for the misappropriation of trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: Volunteer further agrees that upon termination of service with CCPDT, Volunteer shall return within ten (10) business days all documents, files, electronic or otherwise, and property comprising Confidential Information of CCPDT and all copies, electronic or otherwise, thereof relating in any way to the Certification Council for Professional Dog Trainers’ business, or in any way obtained during the course of service. Volunteer further agrees that no copies, electronic or otherwise, notes or abstracts of the foregoing shall be retained and that all information will be returned not destroyed.
perturbation.changed_text: Volunteer agrees that upon termination of service with CCPDT, Volunteer should return documents and files containing Confidential Information, as reasonably practical. Volunteer should also endeavor to not retain copies of Confidential Information. Destruction of information is encouraged where feasible.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall return' to 'should return' and adding 'as reasonably practical' weakens the obligation to return all confidential information upon termination. Changing 'shall be retained' to 'should endeavor to not retain' and encouraging destruction 'where feasible' further diminishes the requirement and introduces subjectivity.
perturbation.law_explanation: By weakening the obligation to return and destroy confidential information, especially electronic data, the modified text could potentially enable a volunteer to unlawfully access stored electronic communications after termination, conflicting with the Stored Communications Act. Depending on the type of information, failure to return information or continuing access to it could violate the act.

perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens the obligation to return and not retain confidential information by using 'should return' and 'should endeavor not to retain', which contrasts with the original's 'shall return' and 'shall be retained'. Additionally, encouraging destruction 'where feasible' introduces subjectivity. The Law Explanation suggests that weakening the obligation to return information could lead to unlawful access to stored electronic communications under the Stored Communications Act. 18 USC 2701, provided in Scraped Law Snippet 2, addresses '[u]nlawful access to stored communications' which is created when a party '[i]ntentionally exceeds an authorization to access that facility'. If a volunteer continues to access confidential information after their service ends, this would possibly violate this act.

perturbation.original_text: If a situation arises where it is unclear as to whether a conflict of interest exists, Volunteer shall discuss the issue with the President of the Certification Council for Professional Dog Trainers (or the Vice President if the Volunteer is the President).
perturbation.changed_text: If a situation possibly involves a conflict of interest, the Volunteer may consider discussing the issue with the President of the Certification Council for Professional Dog Trainers, if convenient.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall discuss' to 'may consider discussing...if convenient' makes the reporting of potential conflicts of interest optional and subject to the volunteer's convenience. This removes a mandatory step, creating ambiguity about when a conflict should be reported.
perturbation.law_explanation: While this section governs financial disclosures and conflict reporting for publicly traded companies, and is only indirectly applicable, the principle of mandatory reporting of potential conflicts of interest is similar. By making conflict reporting optional, the modified text weakens ethical obligations and increases the risk of undisclosed conflicts, which is contrary to the spirit of the Sarbanes-Oxley Act that promotes transparency and accountability.

perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates discussion of potential conflicts of interest ('shall discuss'), while the changed text makes such discussion optional and dependent on the volunteer's convenience ('may consider discussing...if convenient'). This change directly weakens the obligation to report conflicts, creating ambiguity about when such reporting is necessary. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 pertains to financial disclosures for publicly traded companies and is not directly applicable, the principle of mandatory reporting of potential conflicts is relevant. The change introduces ambiguity that undermines ethical obligations, which contradicts the general spirit of transparency and accountability. By changing 'shall' to 'may consider if convenient' the contract could easily be interpreted as not requiring a conflict of interest to be reported if it is not convenient for the volunteer. This undermines the purpose of the clause.
